title: Probabilistic size-and-shape functional mixed models

reviews.summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.
reviews.strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.
reviews.weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.
reviews.questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.
reviews.limitations: The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.

Strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.

Weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.

Questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.

Limitations:
The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Rating:
7
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

The paper addresses the challenging problem of estimating a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models when observations contain both phase (x-axis) and amplitude (y-axis) variability. The key innovation is the use of a norm-preserving action of phase functions on the Hilbert space L², which allows the authors to reliably recover the "size-and-shape" geometric property of µ rather than attempting to recover µ itself. The authors develop a Bayesian hierarchical model with appropriate priors on phase functions and implement posterior inference via MCMC. Evaluation includes both simulated examples and applications to real functional data from growth curves and electrocardiogram signals.

## Strengths

The paper makes a compelling mathematical contribution by introducing the norm-preserving action as an alternative to the more commonly used value-preserving action in functional data analysis. The mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between these two approaches are illuminating and well-presented. The geometric intuition provided in Section 2, particularly through Figure 1(c) and the discussion of how the norm-preserving action Dγ relates to space-time diffeomorphisms, effectively motivates why size-and-shape preservation is a more realistic and recoverable target than classical shape recovery.

The paper does an excellent job of positioning its contributions within the broader context of shape analysis. The connection to landmark-based shape analysis (Section 3, equation 2) provides valuable historical perspective and demonstrates that the proposed approach is a natural extension of well-established statistical principles to the functional data setting. This theoretical grounding significantly strengthens the paper's contribution beyond being a purely methodological development.

The practical implementation strategies are well-designed. The use of basis function approximations (modified Fourier basis for fixed effects, B-spline basis for random effects) demonstrates good understanding of computational trade-offs. The paper explicitly addresses dimension reduction through these basis representations and provides clear justification for the choice of different basis types. The detailed MCMC algorithm in Appendix E and the adaptive proposal distributions show careful attention to computational feasibility.

The paper's treatment of the Lie group structure of phase functions is mathematically sophisticated. The use of the group composition operation in defining proposal distributions (Appendix D) is elegant and theoretically principled. The two prior models (PM1 and PM2) provide a useful spectrum from computationally simple (one-parameter family) to more flexible (Dirichlet process-based) specifications.

The extensive numerical experiments demonstrate the method's practical utility. The inclusion of both simulated data generated from the proposed model and data generated from the warpMix model shows that the proposed approach performs well even when the underlying data generation process differs from the assumed model. The sensitivity analyses in Appendix H provide useful insights into robustness to hyperparameter specification.

## Weaknesses

### Comparison and Benchmarking Issues

The comparisons to warpMix are problematic in several important respects. Most critically, the noise variance parameters are not comparable across methods. The proposed Bayesian model uses σ²_c = 0.25 and σ² = 0.1 in simulations (Section 4.1, Example 1), while the default noise variance in warpMix is at the level of 10⁻³. This represents a 100-1000 fold difference in assumed noise levels. The authors acknowledge this indirectly by using different variance specifications in Example 2 (σ² = 0.0001 for warpMix-generated data), but they do not systematically investigate how this affects fair comparison. It is unclear whether the superior performance of the proposed method in Table 1 is due to the methodological innovations or simply due to these different noise assumptions. A more principled approach would involve matching the assumed noise levels across methods or at least explicitly documenting how the noise variance affects the comparison.

Furthermore, the paper cites substantial prior work by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues that also employs norm-preserving actions and similar geometric principles for functional data analysis. While the authors reference works such as Srivastava and Klassen [2016] and Kurtek et al. [2011, 2012], there is no systematic comparison to these approaches. The paper would be strengthened by explicit comparisons to the shape analysis methods from this literature, particularly since some of these methods also target geometric properties of functions rather than pointwise recovery. The comparison in Appendix A to Cheng et al. [2016] (BRFC) is helpful but limited to a single Bayesian baseline. A more comprehensive comparison strategy would help establish where this work stands relative to the broader landscape of geometric functional data analysis.

The default parameter settings used for warpMix may not represent the best performance of that method. While using default parameters provides reproducibility, a more thorough evaluation would involve tuning warpMix's parameters to the specific datasets and noise levels being considered. This is particularly important given the noise variance discrepancies mentioned above.

### Evaluation and Practical Utility

The evaluation strategy relies heavily on visual assessment and simple pointwise error metrics (Δμ in Table 1). While Figure 4 shows visually appealing results for the Berkeley and PQRST datasets, the assessment that the estimate "contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data" (page 9) is subjective. Without downstream task evaluation, it remains unclear whether the recovered size-and-shape estimates are actually useful in practice. 

The paper would be significantly strengthened by including at least one downstream application demonstrating practical utility. For instance, the authors could:
- Perform classification tasks on the recovered mean functions (e.g., distinguish between healthy and diseased ECG patterns using the estimated PQRST mean)
- Use the estimated means as templates for improved registration or alignment of new data
- Demonstrate that the uncertainty quantification (credible intervals) is well-calibrated and useful for downstream inference
- Show that the recovered size-and-shape estimates generalize better to held-out test data compared to alternatives

Such evaluations would move beyond "the estimate looks reasonable" to "the estimate is demonstrably useful for solving real problems." This is particularly important given that the paper makes claims about "superior" performance over state-of-the-art methods. Superiority in visual appearance is not the same as superiority in practical utility.

The sensitivity analysis in Appendix H is helpful but could be extended. While the authors show robustness to over- and under-specification of Bf, Br, and θγ, they do not thoroughly investigate sensitivity to other modeling choices. For example, what happens when the true underlying data generating process violates the Gaussian error assumption? How sensitive are results to the choice between modified Fourier and B-spline bases? The data-driven FPCA basis comparison in Appendix G is interesting but the explanation for why FPCA performs poorly could be more thorough.

### Basis Function Representation and Flexibility

The reliance on pre-specified basis functions (modified Fourier or B-splines) introduces a modeling choice that deserves more investigation. While the authors argue that the norm-preserving action Dγ "rotates the basis system toward the data," this does not fully address the question of whether the chosen basis family is appropriate for the application. The comparison to FPCA in Appendix G shows that data-driven bases can perform poorly, but this raises questions about what makes a basis appropriate.

The paper would benefit from more explicit guidance on basis selection in practice. When should practitioners choose modified Fourier basis versus B-splines? The authors mention that "in some cases where the underlying μ is expected to exhibit more local features, we use the B-spline basis" (page 5), but this guidance is vague. How can practitioners determine a priori whether their data will exhibit local features? A more systematic approach to basis selection would improve the practical applicability of the method.

The question of whether the method could be implemented without basis function approximations is raised but not thoroughly addressed. While the authors briefly mention using "samples" directly, they do not explore the practical and theoretical implications. Direct functional representations might avoid basis selection issues but could introduce other computational challenges. This discussion would be valuable for practitioners considering implementation.

### Computational Efficiency and Scalability

The computational cost is non-trivial. As reported in Section 5, obtaining 100,000 posterior samples after burn-in requires approximately 93-111 minutes on a 6-core server for n=30 functions with T=50 discretization points. This scales unfavorably as sample size or discretization increases. For a dataset with n=93 observations (Berkeley data), the computational cost would be substantially higher, yet the authors do not provide timing information for these real data examples.

The authors acknowledge that "there is room for improvement of efficiency in the MCMC computations" but do not provide concrete suggestions beyond mentioning that "alternatives may be explored." This is a significant limitation for practical application to larger datasets. More specifically:
- How does computation scale with sample size n and discretization points T?
- Would variational inference or approximate Bayesian methods provide substantial speedups?
- Are there opportunities to parallelize over observations or use other computational strategies?

The paper would be strengthened by either (1) providing timing results for all examples including real data,

reviews.summary: The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.
reviews.strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.
reviews.weaknesses:
## Significance:



1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).
    2. The explanation in footnote 1 is a bit hand-wavy and it would be great if rigorous proof could be provided or referred to.

## Novelty:


The idea that the parameterization of the model is not minimal and therefore there is the ambiguity of having an equivalence class of solutions has been there for a long time, as the authors also point out in equation (2). Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.
reviews.questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?
reviews.limitations: See above

Questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task notoriously difficult. The authors argue that while exact recovery of µ may be impossible under traditional frameworks, it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain norm-preserving transformations. The paper proposes a Bayesian functional mixed model framework that leverages the isometric action of phase functions on the Hilbert space L² to achieve this goal. The formulation is justified through careful mathematical exposition and supported by numerical experiments on both synthetic and real data, with comparisons to the state-of-the-art warpMix method.

## Strengths

1. **Clarity and presentation**: The presentation overall is clear, self-contained, and precise. While these properties might be considered standard expectations, they are unfortunately rare in contemporary papers, making their presence particularly valuable. The paper successfully navigates complex functional data analysis concepts while maintaining accessibility. The mathematical exposition in Section 2, which introduces phase functions and the distinction between value-preserving and norm-preserving actions, is carefully structured to build intuition before diving into technical details. The use of Figure 1(c) to visually illustrate the difference between these two actions is particularly effective.

2. **Rigorous mathematical framework**: The formulation is carefully backed up by detailed justifications for each modeling choice, including the selection of basis functions (modified Fourier basis for the fixed effect, B-spline basis for random effects), prior distributions (two tractable prior models on the space of phase functions Γ), and proposal distributions for MCMC sampling. The connection to statistical shape analysis of landmark configurations (Equation 2) provides strong theoretical motivation for the proposed approach. The detailed derivations in the appendices (particularly Appendices C, D, and E) demonstrate the authors' commitment to rigor and reproducibility.

3. **Comprehensive numerical validation**: The paper includes extensive numerical experiments that thoroughly investigate the utility of the proposed model. The experiments progress logically from simulated data generated under the proposed model (Example 1) to more challenging scenarios where data are generated under the competing warpMix model (Example 2), providing evidence of robustness. The real data applications (Berkeley growth rate data and PQRST complexes) demonstrate practical utility, and the supplementary materials provide results on five additional datasets. The sensitivity analyses in Appendix H, which assess robustness to hyperparameter misspecification, strengthen the empirical validation.

4. **Novel geometric perspective**: The paper introduces a fresh geometric perspective on functional mixed effects modeling by leveraging the norm-preserving action of phase functions. The interpretation of phase variation as automatic identification of data-optimal rotations of the chosen orthonormal basis (Contribution 3) is conceptually interesting and provides practical modeling flexibility, particularly when using a small number of basis functions (as demonstrated in Figure 6 of Appendix B).

## Weaknesses

### Significance

1. **Limited motivation for practical applicability**: The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a norm-preserving manner (Equations 3 and 4). However, the significance of such a model remains unclear from a practical standpoint. Several important questions are inadequately addressed:
   
   a. **Lack of real-world justification**: Are there concrete practical applications in which we have principled reasons to believe that phase functions act on the fixed effect µ in a norm-preserving way rather than the more traditional value-preserving way? The paper motivates the problem through the Berkeley growth data and PQRST complexes, yet neither example provides compelling evidence that the norm-preserving action is the appropriate model for these applications. In fact, the PQRST data appears to have natural features (the characteristic QRS complex pattern) that one might expect to be preserved under value-preserving transformations. The authors acknowledge that phase variation in real applications is typically handled through pre-processing registration, but they do not explain why simultaneous joint modeling with the norm-preserving action should be preferred in practice.
   
   b. **Incomplete comparison of modeling paradigms**: While the paper provides a comparison between value-preserving and norm-preserving actions in Section 2 and Appendix B, the practical implications of choosing one over the other are not fully explored. The statement that the equivalence class under norm-preserving action is "larger" (lines 142-147) is meant to explain why recovery is more reliable, but this argument would benefit from more thorough empirical validation. For instance, the paper could include experiments where data are generated under a value-preserving model and show how much the norm-preserving model degrades in performance, or vice versa.

2. **Limited real data comparisons and ground truth issues**: The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) is somewhat limited and raises important questions about model evaluation. When comparing the two methods on the Berkeley data, the proposed method recovers two growth spurts while warpMix recovers only one. However, without ground truth, it is difficult to assess which method provides a more accurate representation of the underlying biological process. The paper claims superiority based on visual inspection and the fact that the recovered pattern "agrees with previous literature," but this is somewhat circular reasoning—the literature itself may have employed similar methods. More rigorous evaluation metrics or validation approaches (e.g., cross-validation, posterior predictive checks, or expert domain knowledge) would strengthen the claims. Furthermore, the fact that warpMix fails entirely on the PQRST data suggests that the two methods may be optimized for different problem settings, and it is unclear whether the proposed method is genuinely superior or simply better suited to this particular dataset.

3. **Computational efficiency not addressed**: The paper explicitly states in Section 5 that "We do not make any claims on efficiency of the computational algorithms." This is a significant limitation. The reported computational time of approximately 93-111 minutes for n=30 functions discretized at T=50 points is substantial, particularly when compared to the computational requirements of warpMix or other frequentist approaches. For practitioners considering adoption of this method, computational scalability is a critical concern. The paper should provide more detailed discussion of how the method scales with sample size n, dimension T, and the complexity of the phase function prior (comparing PM1 and PM2). Without such information, it is difficult to assess the practical utility of the method for larger datasets.

### Clarity

1. **Insufficient explanation of the equivalence class argument (lines 142-147)**: The claim that "the equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is 'larger' than the one under the value-preserving action" requires more rigorous exposition. The explanation provided is not sufficiently clear:
   
   a. **Counterargument not adequately addressed**: The reviewer's concern that the same argument could be applied to value-preserving actions (for any g and γ(t), one can find f(t) := g(γ⁻¹(t)) such that f(γ(t)) = g(t)) is valid and deserves direct engagement. While the authors reference Example 6.5.2 in Bogachev (1998) regarding pushforward measures, this reference alone does not constitute a satisfactory explanation for a reader unfamiliar with measure-theoretic arguments.
   
   b. **Measure-theoretic foundation needed**: The claim about larger equivalence classes relies on pushforward measures and non-degenerate measures with support in L². The paper should provide either a rigorous proof or at least a more intuitive explanation of why this measure-theoretic argument supports the claim that norm-preserving actions lead to more reliable recovery. What specific properties of the pushforward measure make the equivalence class "larger" in a meaningful sense? How does this translate to improved recovery in the presence of noise?
   
   c. **Empirical validation of the equivalence class claim**: While Appendix B provides some empirical evidence (Figure 6 shows lower residuals when allowing norm-preserving rotations), this does not directly validate the claim about equivalence class sizes. More direct analysis—perhaps through explicit calculation of the measure of equivalence classes under different actions—would strengthen this argument.

2. **Unclear introduction and problem motivation**: The introduction presents multiple related problems in a way that obscures the central contribution:
   
   a. **Conflicting problem statements**: The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, emphasizing that the sample size is smaller than the observation dimension, necessitating prior constraints. However, the model in Equation (1) is presented as rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery. This creates confusion about whether the core problem is dimensionality or phase variability.
   
   b. **Unmotivated problem escalation**: The second paragraph then introduces phase variability (γᵢ), which makes the problem "even more complicated and harder to estimate" (as noted by the reviewer). The paper does not adequately explain why phase variability is a necessary consideration for the Berkeley data or other applications. What evidence suggests that phase variation is present in these datasets? How sensitive
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.

Strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.

Weaknesses:
## Significance:



1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).
    2. The explanation in footnote 1 is a bit hand-wavy and it would be great if rigorous proof could be provided or referred to.

## Novelty:


The idea that the parameterization of the model is not minimal and therefore there is the ambiguity of having an equivalence class of solutions has been there for a long time, as the authors also point out in equation (2). Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.

Questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?

Limitations:
See above

Rating:
7

Summary:
The paper considers uncertainty quantification for one-dimensional regression tasks, where the observations are noisy. A rather advanced additive model considering invariances under space-time unitary transformations is proposted. Numerical experiments demonstrate the superiority of the approach over other state-of-the-art methods.

Strengths:
- Due to the strong modeling assumptions, on synthethic datasets, the method can recover the true signal even in situations where there is lots of noise and classical methods (such as GPs) may fail. 
- The methods seems to be useful to analyze the Berkley growth spurt data set.

Weaknesses:
- It is unclear whether the work will be of interest to the wider machine learning audience and the considered topic and datasets feel more of a niche. 
- Despite the well-written introduction, the paper was quite difficult to understand due to the advanced math and notations -- after reading it I am still quite unsure why I should really care about functional mixed models and their Bayesian inference.

Questions:
I am slightly puzzled as of why NeurIPS was chosen as a venue for this paper - the work may not get the appreciation here it deserves. I spent quite some time on trying to understand the paper, but its motivations and applications to wider machine learning remain unclear. Perhaps the paper may be better suited towards a statistics journal, such as the ones referenced often in the paper?   If more general motivations are clarified, I am considering to change my score, but accessibility issues are remaining.

Limitations:
All limitations are adequately addressed.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

The paper proposes a Bayesian functional mixed model for uncertainty quantification in one-dimensional functional regression tasks with noisy observations. The key innovation lies in the introduction of a size-and-shape preserving geometric perspective through the use of norm-preserving actions of phase functions on function spaces. This approach employs advanced mathematical machinery involving unitary transformations and Hilbert space rotations to separate amplitude variation from phase variation in functional data. Numerical experiments on both synthetic and real datasets demonstrate the approach's superiority compared to existing state-of-the-art methods, particularly the frequentist warpMix approach of Claeskels et al. (2021).

## Strengths

- **Strong theoretical foundation with clear mathematical exposition**: The paper builds on rigorous mathematical foundations from functional data analysis and shape statistics. The connection drawn between landmark-based shape analysis (where rotations and translations preserve size-and-shape) and functional data analysis through the norm-preserving action is conceptually elegant and well-motivated. The authors carefully explain the distinction between value-preserving, area-preserving, and norm-preserving actions of phase functions, with Figure 1(c) providing particularly helpful intuition.

- **Robust recovery under challenging conditions**: Due to the strong modeling assumptions inherent in the framework, the method demonstrates remarkable ability to recover the true signal even in situations where there is substantial noise and classical methods such as Gaussian processes may fail. In Example 1 (Section 4.1), where data is generated from the proposed model, the posterior mean estimates closely match the ground truth across both simulated scenarios (PM1 and PM2), while competing methods produce visibly distorted estimates.

- **Practical utility on real datasets**: The method appears to be genuinely useful for analyzing real functional data, as demonstrated on the Berkeley growth rate dataset. The estimated size-and-shape of µ reveals two distinct growth spurts (a small initial one followed by a larger pubertal one) that align with prior literature. The PQRST complex analysis also demonstrates the method's ability to capture sharp local features representative of ECG signals, recovering patterns that the warpMix method failed to identify entirely.

- **Comprehensive experimental validation**: The paper provides extensive numerical experiments including simulations where data is generated from both the proposed model and the competing warpMix model. Table 1 and Table 2 provide quantitative comparisons, with the proposed Bayesian models achieving lower estimation errors across multiple scenarios. The inclusion of sensitivity analyses in Appendix H examining robustness to hyperparameter misspecification strengthens the empirical validation.

- **Careful treatment of computational details**: The authors provide thorough documentation of their MCMC algorithm, including detailed specifications of proposal distributions (Appendix D), derivations of Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratios, and diagnostic plots (Appendix F) that demonstrate convergence. The reproducibility checklist and supplementary code with step-by-step instructions facilitate verification of results.

## Weaknesses

- **Limited relevance to broader machine learning community**: The work addresses a specialized problem within functional data analysis that may not resonate with the wider machine learning audience. While functional data analysis is an established subfield, the specific focus on Bayesian inference for mixed models with phase variation represents a relatively narrow contribution. The datasets considered (growth curves, ECG signals, pinch force measurements) are domain-specific and may not illustrate the broader applicability of the approach. A reader working on, for instance, computer vision, natural language processing, or large-scale machine learning systems may struggle to identify relevant connections or potential applications of this framework.

- **Significant accessibility challenges due to mathematical sophistication**: Despite the well-written introduction that provides good intuition, the paper becomes increasingly difficult to follow as it progresses into the technical details. The extensive use of advanced mathematical notation—including infinite-dimensional Lie groups (Γ), functional Hilbert spaces (L²), unitary operators (Dγ), and their properties—creates substantial barriers to understanding for readers without specialized background in functional analysis or shape statistics. The core geometric insight about norm-preserving actions, while elegant, requires considerable mathematical maturity to fully appreciate. Many readers may find themselves reading the introduction, becoming interested, but then struggling through Sections 2 and 3 where the mathematical abstraction increases substantially. This accessibility issue is particularly concerning for a venue like NeurIPS where the audience is more heterogeneous in mathematical background than specialized statistics conferences.

- **Unclear broader motivation and impact**: Beyond demonstrating that the method works well on specific datasets, the paper does not articulate compelling reasons why practitioners in machine learning should care about this particular approach or what new capabilities it enables. The motivating question—"what properties of µ may be reliably recovered?"—is answered in the specific context of size-and-shape, but the paper does not explain why this particular geometric property is the most relevant one for practitioners or how it compares to other possible geometric properties one might consider. The discussion in Section 5 mentions potential extensions to curves in Rd and parameterized surfaces, but these remain largely speculative and do not strengthen the case for the current contribution.

- **Computational efficiency concerns**: While the authors acknowledge in Section 5 that "there is room for improvement of efficiency in the MCMC computations," the reported computational times are substantial. For n=30 functions discretized at T=50 points, the MCMC algorithm requires approximately 93-111 minutes on a computing server with 6 parallel Intel Xeon CPUs to generate 100,000 posterior samples. This computational burden may limit practical applicability, particularly for larger datasets or more complex functional data problems. The paper does not provide detailed scalability analysis or discuss how computational time grows with sample size n or discretization level T, which would be important for practitioners considering adoption of this method.

- **Limited theoretical justification**: The paper explicitly states in Section 5 that "what is lacking is theoretical support for the same, and this is work in progress." While the authors appropriately acknowledge this limitation, the absence of theoretical guarantees regarding identifiability, consistency, or convergence rates of posterior estimates weakens the contribution. For instance, while the paper argues that the larger equivalence class under norm-preserving action (point (iii) in Section 2) helps with reliable recovery compared to value-preserving action, this intuition is not formalized theoretically. Theoretical results characterizing when and why the size-and-shape of µ can be reliably recovered would significantly strengthen the paper.

- **Restrictive modeling assumptions**: The paper makes several simplifying assumptions that may limit applicability. The assumption of common discretization across all functional observations, while acknowledged as relaxable, is not addressed in the experiments. The Gaussian likelihood and independence assumptions on the discretized error process are standard but somewhat restrictive. More importantly, the choice of basis functions (modified Fourier basis or B-splines) and the number of basis functions (Bf and Br) are specified a priori rather than estimated from data. While Appendix H demonstrates robustness to over-specification, the need to pre-specify these quantities introduces a degree of subjectivity into the analysis.

## Questions and Clarifications

- **Venue appropriateness**: I am puzzled as to why NeurIPS was chosen as the primary venue for this work. The paper makes a solid contribution to functional data analysis and Bayesian statistics, but the specialized mathematical machinery, domain-specific applications, and limited connections to broader machine learning problems suggest that the work may receive greater appreciation and impact at a specialized statistics venue such as the Journal of the American Statistical Association, Biometrika, or the Electronic Journal of Statistics, where the audience has deeper familiarity with functional data analysis methods. While NeurIPS has increasingly included statistical methodology papers, the accessibility issues and narrow scope of applications may limit the paper's visibility and impact within the machine learning community. Have the authors considered submitting to a statistics-focused venue where the mathematical sophistication would be better aligned with reader expectations?

- **Practical guidance for practitioners**: Could the authors provide clearer guidance on when practitioners should use this method versus simpler alternatives? For instance, when is the computational cost of MCMC sampling justified compared to the frequentist warpMix approach? What characteristics of a functional dataset would make the size-and-shape perspective particularly valuable? The paper would benefit from a decision tree or practical guidelines for method selection.

- **Comparison scope**: The numerical experiments compare primarily against warpMix (Claeskels et al., 2021) and briefly against the Bayesian registration approach of Cheng et al. (2016). Have the authors considered comparisons with other modern functional data analysis methods, such as those based on Gaussian process functional regression or more recent deep learning approaches for functional data? Such comparisons might help situate this work within the broader landscape of functional data analysis methods.

- **Identifiability and confounding**: While the paper addresses the fundamental challenge that exact recovery of µ is not possible due to confounding between phase and amplitude variation, could the authors provide more formal analysis of identifiability? Specifically, under what conditions on the prior distributions for γi and vi can one guarantee that the posterior distribution of the size-and-shape of µ is identifiable? The paper relies on informative priors to regularize inference, but the precise role these priors play in achieving

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

This paper addresses the challenging problem of reliably recovering the size-and-shape of an unknown fixed effect function μ in a functional mixed model through Bayesian inference. The authors propose a novel approach that samples from and summarizes the posterior distribution of μ in a functional mixed model with random object-level phase and amplitude components. A key distinguishing feature of their work is that they do not assume a finite-rank covariance structure on the error process, which represents a departure from restrictive assumptions made in prior work. The paper employs the norm-preserving action of phase functions to define a geometric property—size-and-shape—that can be reliably recovered even when the exact topological features of μ cannot be identified due to confounding with phase and amplitude variability.

---

## Strengths

1) **Addresses a challenging and important problem**: This paper tackles a notoriously difficult task in functional data analysis—the reliable recovery of distributional properties of functions in the presence of simultaneous phase and amplitude variation. The authors correctly identify and articulate the fundamental identifiability challenge: variations along the x-axis (phase) and y-axis (amplitude) are inherently confounded with additive measurement error and cannot be completely disentangled without further constraints. By shifting focus from recovering the exact function μ to recovering its size-and-shape, the paper provides a principled and pragmatic solution to this identifiability problem. This perspective is both novel and well-motivated, as demonstrated through the Berkeley growth rate and PQRST complex examples.

2) **Theoretically interesting mathematical framework**: The mathematical development is sophisticated and well-grounded. The derivation of the Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio (Appendix D) is particularly thorough and demonstrates careful treatment of the group structure of phase functions. The connection between the norm-preserving action D_γ and unitary transformations of the Hilbert space is elegantly presented. The authors effectively leverage the Lie group structure of phase functions Γ in both the specification of prior distributions and the design of proposal distributions for MCMC sampling. The mathematical exposition in Section 2, which distinguishes between value-preserving, area-preserving, and norm-preserving actions, provides valuable geometric intuition. The isometric property of the operator D_γ and its interpretation as an infinite-dimensional rotation of L² is well-articulated and provides a solid theoretical foundation for the modeling choices.

3) **Comprehensive convergence analysis through numerical experiments**: The authors provide convincing empirical evidence of MCMC convergence through extensive diagnostic plots presented in Appendix F. Trace plots for all model parameters across multiple examples (simulated data under PM1 and PM2, Berkeley growth data, and PQRST complexes) demonstrate good mixing and convergence to the stationary posterior distribution. The posterior samples cluster tightly around posterior means, and the inclusion of ground truth values (where applicable) in the diagnostic plots allows for visual verification that the algorithm recovers known quantities accurately. This empirical validation is particularly important given the complexity of the MCMC algorithm and the high-dimensional parameter space.

4) **Extensive numerical validation and superior performance**: The paper demonstrates consistent superiority over state-of-the-art methods through carefully designed experiments. In Example 1 (Section 4.1), when data is generated from the proposed model, the method reliably recovers both μ and the underlying phase functions under both PM1 and PM2 specifications. In Example 2, which deliberately uses the warpMix generative model (value-preserving action) rather than the norm-preserving action employed by the proposed method, the proposed Bayesian models still achieve competitive or superior performance. For instance, Model 2-F achieves an 82% error reduction compared to warpMix for μ₂. The comparison in Table 1 shows that across three different fixed effect functions with varying complexity, at least one of the proposed models outperforms warpMix in all cases. On real data (Berkeley and PQRST), the method recovers interpretable estimates with reasonable uncertainty quantification, and warpMix fails entirely on the PQRST dataset.

5) **Flexible modeling framework with multiple prior specifications**: The paper provides two complementary prior models for phase functions (PM1 and PM2), allowing practitioners to balance computational tractability against modeling flexibility. PM1's one-parameter family (Equation 7) provides computational efficiency while PM2's nonparametric Dirichlet process-based approach offers greater flexibility to capture complex phase variation including inflection points. This flexibility is demonstrated empirically: the Berkeley data analysis uses PM1 while the PQRST analysis uses PM2 due to the presence of inflection points in the estimated phase functions. The authors also explore the impact of basis function choices (modified Fourier vs. B-splines) across different examples, demonstrating that the framework accommodates different basis selections appropriately.

6) **Thorough and transparent documentation**: The supplementary material is exceptionally comprehensive. Detailed derivations of the marginal likelihood (Appendix C), complete specifications of proposal distributions and MCMC algorithm (Appendices D and E), sensitivity analyses for hyperparameter misspecification (Appendix H), and results on five additional real datasets (Appendix I) provide substantial supporting material. The authors honestly acknowledge limitations and provide extensive diagnostic plots. The inclusion of a README file with step-by-step instructions for reproducibility and the provision of code and data demonstrate a strong commitment to scientific transparency and reproducibility.

---

## Weaknesses

1) **Ambiguity regarding prior model selection for different applications**: While the paper provides two prior models for phase functions (PM1 and PM2), there is insufficient guidance on how practitioners should choose between them for new applications. The paper states that "one needs to choose the number of basis functions to model μ and vᵢ. This choice depends on the application of interest" (Section 3.1), but this guidance remains somewhat vague. Although Appendix H provides sensitivity analysis showing robustness to over-specification of basis functions, the practical challenge of selecting appropriate prior models a priori is not thoroughly addressed. For instance, how should a practitioner decide between PM1 and PM2 before analyzing a new dataset? The Berkeley example uses PM1 while PQRST uses PM2, but the decision criteria are not made explicit. The paper would benefit from more concrete guidelines or diagnostic tools for prior model selection. Additionally, the choice of the concentration parameter θ_γ = 30 in PM2 appears somewhat arbitrary, and while Appendix H explores θ_γ ∈ {1, 30, 100}, more guidance on selecting this hyperparameter for new applications would strengthen the practical utility of the method.

2) **Insufficient analysis of parameter sensitivity and computational efficiency**: While Appendix H provides sensitivity analysis for hyperparameters B_f, B_r, and θ_γ, the analysis is limited to a single simulated dataset (Example 1 from Section 4.1). The robustness of these conclusions to different data characteristics—such as varying sample sizes, different levels of noise, or different functional complexity—remains unexplored. The computational cost discussion in Section 5 is brief and somewhat dismissive: the authors state "There is room for improvement of efficiency in the MCMC computations" but do not provide detailed analysis of computational complexity or scalability. For n = 30 functions at T = 50 time points, the method requires approximately 93-111 minutes of computation time using 6 parallel CPUs. The paper does not provide guidance on how computation time scales with n, T, or the number of basis functions, nor does it discuss the memory requirements in detail. This is a significant practical limitation that deserves more thorough analysis, particularly for practitioners considering application to larger datasets. The authors should provide computational complexity analysis or at least empirical scaling results across different problem sizes.

3) **Limited theoretical justification for size-and-shape recoverability**: While the paper provides good intuitive explanation and empirical evidence that size-and-shape can be reliably recovered, formal theoretical guarantees are absent. The authors explicitly acknowledge this in Section 5: "What is lacking is theoretical support for the same, and this is work in progress." This is a notable limitation. The paper would be strengthened by at least a theoretical characterization of when and why size-and-shape is identifiable while the full function is not, perhaps through formal identifiability analysis or asymptotic consistency results. The larger equivalence class argument in Section 2 (point iii) provides intuition but lacks formal justification. Specifically, the claim that "the measure of an equivalence class under the pushforward of a non-degenerate measure with support in L² under π is larger when ∼ pertains to norm-preserving action as opposed to value-preserving one" is supported only by a reference to Example 6.5.2 in Bogachev (1998) rather than direct proof or formal statement. Theoretical results characterizing the posterior consistency or contraction rates for μ under the proposed model would substantially strengthen the contribution.

4) **Incomplete comparison with neural network-based function recovery methods**: The paper does not address how the proposed Bayesian approach compares to

Summary:
For the problem of the reliable recovery of a fixed effect function $\mu$, this paper focuses on sampling from and summarizing the posterior distribution of a fixed effect function $\mu$ in a functional mixed model with random object-level phase and amplitude components, without a finite-rank covariance assumption on the error process.

----
Post-rebuttal: The rebuttal addressed most of my concerns and I would like to raise my score.

Strengths:
1）	This paper tries to solve a challenging task, recovering the size-and-shape of an unknown function by sampling. 
2）	The theoretical analysis is interesting, e.g., Derivation of Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio.
3）	The authors also give the convergence analysis by numerical experiments.

Weaknesses:
1)	It seems that we should give different prior models for different functions.
2)	The parameter sensitivity of $\theta$ and the running time of the proposed method should be given.

Questions:
1)	Neural networks can also recover functions by sampling. What are the advantages of the proposed method compared to neural networks?
2)	How can we recover arbitrary by a fixed model in Eq.(3)?

Rating:
5

reviews.strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.
reviews.weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.
reviews.questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?
reviews.limitations: The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.
