title: Sourcerer: Sample-based Maximum Entropy Source Distribution Estimation

reviews.summary:
This paper deals with the problem of identifying the distribution of a source variable $s$ that generates observations $x$.

## The problem

The authors propose to minimize a classical objective consisting of two terms: 

1. A reconstruction term, that encourages the recovered source distribution to induce a distribution over observations that is close to the data distribution, in a certain divergence (here, the sliced Wasserstein)

2. A regularization term, that encourages the recovered source distribution to be close to a reference distribution, in a certain divergence (here, the reverse KL). 

Many different source distributions can minimize the first term alone, which motivates the second term to contrain the solution space. 

## The probability model

The probability model $p(x)$ can be expressed in terms of two other model distributions: the source distribution $p(s)$ and the likelihood $p(x | s)$.  

Term (1) uses a (sliced) Wasserstein divergence. Computing this divergence requires data samples and samples from the model in two steps:  $s \sim p(s)$ then $x | s \sim p(x | s)$. Differentiating through this divergence requires the probability model, and therefore $p(s)$ and $p(x | s)$, to be samplable and the sampling process should be differentiable. 

Term (2) uses a reverse KL divergence. Computing this divergence requires evaluating the densities of the model and reference distributions, as well as samples from the model in two steps:  $s \sim p(s)$ then $x | s \sim p(x | s)$. Differentiating through this divergence requires the probability model, and therefore $p(s)$ and $p(x | s)$, to be samplable and the sampling process should be differentiable. Additionally, the model density should be known or else a differentiable approximation of the divergence (the authors use the Kozachenko-Leonenko estimator) should be available. 

## Numerics

The authors validate their method using synthetic data where the source is known, and real data where the observations are neurophyisiological recordings and the likelihood is given by a mechanistic model.
reviews.strengths: The presentation is clear and well-explained.
reviews.weaknesses:
Overall, I have two concerns. 

1. The computational constraints on the model are heavy

The choices of divergences (Sliced-Wasserstein and reverse KL) in the cost function impose a number of computational constraints of the model.

Term 1 in the cost uses a vanilla Sliced-Wasserstein divergence estimator that is sample-based. This means the likelihood model $p(x | s)$ should be samplable and the sampling process should be differentiable. The authors propose to train a differentiable approximator of a black-box simulator. To be fair, the authors acknowledge that this is a general issue beyond their paper. 

Term 2 in the cost uses a vanilla KL divergence estimator which requires knowing the density of the model, but the authors circumvent this by using another estimator named "Kozachenko-Leonenko". Given that the authors already used a sample-based divergence in Term 1, wouldn't using another sample-based divergence in the Term 2 (e.g. the Sliced-Wasserstein again, or an MMD) between the model and reference distributions not add any further computational constraints on the model?

2. The evaluation procedure or goal remains unclear to me

The framework of minimizing a cost made up of a reconstruction and regularization term is well-studied in optimization. Once could imagine two natural evaluation procedures:
- how well the true sources are recovered? But the authors are clear that designing an identifiable probability model where the sources can be recovered (up to acceptable indeterminacies) is not the goal here. 
- how efficient is their method on a new test dataset, as compared to other similar methods?

It seems to be that the authors answer the latter, but it is not clear to me what are the other methods to benchmark against.
reviews.questions: Could the authors address the weaknesses part?
reviews.limitations: Yes.

Questions:
Could the authors address the weaknesses part?

Limitations:

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: Sourcerer

## Summary

This paper addresses the problem of source distribution estimation, wherein the authors seek to identify the distribution of parameters θ (the "source") that, when passed through a simulator with likelihood p(x|θ), generates observations consistent with an empirical dataset. The authors propose Sourcerer, a method that combines two key ideas: (1) targeting the maximum entropy source distribution to resolve the ill-posedness of the problem, and (2) using sample-based divergences, particularly the Sliced-Wasserstein distance, to avoid requiring tractable likelihoods.

The problem is well-motivated by practical applications across scientific disciplines—from unfolding in high-energy physics to population inference in gravitational wave astronomy to the electrophysiology application demonstrated in the paper. The authors correctly identify that without regularization, the source distribution estimation problem can be ill-posed, as multiple distinct source distributions q(θ) can yield the same pushforward distribution q#(x) when passed through a simulator.

## The Proposed Approach

The core contribution is formulated as a regularized optimization problem (Eq. 3-4):

$$\min_{\phi} \lambda D_{\text{KL}}(q_\phi || p) + (1-\lambda) \log(D(q_\phi^{\#}, p_o))$$

where D is a distance metric (Sliced-Wasserstein in this work), q_φ is a neural sampler parameterizing the source distribution, and λ is dynamically scheduled during training. The authors employ Proposition 2.1, which establishes that the maximum entropy source distribution is unique when it exists, providing theoretical justification for their approach. The proof correctly demonstrates that the set of source distributions forms a convex set and entropy is strictly concave, guaranteeing uniqueness of the maximum entropy solution.

The use of a dynamical schedule for λ, starting from λ=1 and decaying to a final value λ>0, is an interesting practical contribution inspired by constrained optimization and reinforcement learning. This encourages the algorithm to first explore high-entropy distributions before increasingly prioritizing data consistency, which appears to help avoid local minima.

## The Probability Model and Computational Constraints

The probability model decomposes as:
$$p(x) = \int p(x|\theta) q(\theta) d\theta$$

where samples are obtained through a two-step process: θ ~ q(θ) followed by x|θ ~ p(x|θ).

### Concern 1: Heavy Computational Constraints

The choices of divergences impose significant computational constraints on the model, and this concern merits careful examination.

**Term 1 (Data Consistency):** The Sliced-Wasserstein divergence in Eq. (5-6) requires:
- Samples from both the empirical distribution p_o(x) and the model pushforward q_φ^#(x)
- Differentiable sampling from the simulator p(x|θ)
- The ability to backpropagate through the sampling process

For black-box simulators without differentiable implementations, the authors propose training a surrogate model (e.g., RealNVP flows for benchmark tasks). While the authors acknowledge this requirement and demonstrate it can be done, it introduces a significant additional computational burden. For example, in the Hodgkin-Huxley experiments (Appendix A.9), training the surrogate MLP on 10^6 parameter-simulation pairs took approximately 20 minutes before source estimation could begin. This is a substantial overhead that practitioners must account for.

The paper would be strengthened by a more thorough discussion of when surrogate training is feasible and when it becomes prohibitive. For instance, how does surrogate quality affect the final source estimates? The authors show results with surrogates for benchmark tasks but do not systematically investigate the degradation in source estimation quality as a function of surrogate approximation error.

**Term 2 (Regularization):** The KL divergence term in Eq. (4) requires evaluating the density p(θ), which is problematic when q_φ is a neural sampler without tractable likelihood. The authors circumvent this using the Kozachenko-Leonenko (KL) entropy estimator, a nearest-neighbor based density estimator. While this is a reasonable approach, it introduces additional computational overhead: computing all-pairs nearest neighbors is O(n log n) in the number of samples, and the estimator's bias and variance properties depend on the sample size and dimensionality.

Here lies a key observation: given that Term 1 already uses a sample-based divergence (Sliced-Wasserstein), why not use another sample-based divergence for Term 2 as well? For instance, one could replace the KL divergence with a Sliced-Wasserstein distance between q_φ(θ) and p(θ), or use Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD). This would eliminate the need for the Kozachenko-Leonenko estimator and avoid the computational cost of nearest-neighbor searches. The authors do briefly explore MMD as an alternative in Figure A4, finding "comparable results," but this is relegated to the appendix and not discussed in the main text. A more thorough investigation of this design choice would strengthen the paper.

The authors write in Section 2.5: "While neural samplers do not have a tractable likelihood, they are faster to evaluate than models with tractable likelihoods." However, the computational cost comparison between neural samplers with Kozachenko-Leonenko entropy estimation versus, say, normalizing flows with tractable likelihoods is not clearly delineated. Table A1 shows that Sourcerer (with regularization) takes ~63.9 seconds for Two Moons vs. ~145.2 seconds for NEB, but this comparison conflates the choice of divergence with the choice of source model. A fairer comparison would isolate these factors.

### Additional Computational Considerations

The paper provides computational resources in Appendix A.10, noting that a single source estimation run for benchmark tasks takes ~30 seconds on an A100 GPU, compared to ~2 minutes for NEB. While Sourcerer is faster, the total pipeline cost (surrogate training + source estimation) can be substantial for complex simulators. For the Hodgkin-Huxley application, the total time is ~20.5 minutes (20 min for surrogate + 0.5 min for source estimation). This is reasonable for a single application but could become prohibitive in scenarios requiring many source estimations.

Furthermore, the scalability to very high-dimensional parameter spaces is not thoroughly explored. The Gaussian Mixture task in Figure A5 is extended to D=25 dimensions, but most other experiments remain in lower dimensions (D≤13 for Hodgkin-Huxley, D≤4 for SIR/Lotka-Volterra). How does the Kozachenko-Leonenko estimator perform in high dimensions? The curse of dimensionality could degrade entropy estimation quality, potentially affecting the method's ability to identify truly high-entropy sources.

## Concern 2: Evaluation Procedure and Benchmarking

The evaluation procedure raises important questions about what the paper is actually demonstrating.

### The Identifiability Question

The authors are explicit that designing an identifiable probability model where sources can be recovered up to acceptable indeterminacies is not the goal. Instead, they focus on the maximum entropy principle as a regularization criterion. However, this raises a conceptual question: if multiple source distributions can produce the same data distribution (the ill-posedness they identify), then how do we know which one is "correct"?

The paper answers this by appealing to the maximum entropy principle—the most "ignorant" distribution is preferable. This is philosophically reasonable and connects to established ideas in statistical mechanics and information theory. However, the practical implications are less clear. In the benchmark experiments (Section 3.1), the authors generate synthetic data from a known "original source distribution" and then try to recover it. Interestingly, for some simulators (IK and SLCP), the estimated source has *higher entropy* than the original source (Figure 3a and A7), despite producing indistinguishable simulations (C2ST accuracy ~50%). The authors note: "This does not contradict our approach: The original source distribution just happens not to be the maximum entropy source for these simulators."

This is a striking observation that deserves more discussion. It suggests that the original source used to generate the data is not the unique maximum entropy source—there exists another source with higher entropy that produces equally valid simulations. This is entirely consistent with the theory, but it raises a practical question: if the true data-generating source is not the maximum entropy source, is maximizing entropy still the right objective? The paper would benefit from a deeper investigation of when and why the maximum entropy source differs from the ground truth, and what implications this has for downstream applications (e.g., using the estimated source as a prior for Bayesian inference).

### Benchmark Comparisons
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper deals with the problem of identifying the distribution of a source variable $s$ that generates observations $x$.

## The problem

The authors propose to minimize a classical objective consisting of two terms: 

1. A reconstruction term, that encourages the recovered source distribution to induce a distribution over observations that is close to the data distribution, in a certain divergence (here, the sliced Wasserstein)

2. A regularization term, that encourages the recovered source distribution to be close to a reference distribution, in a certain divergence (here, the reverse KL). 

Many different source distributions can minimize the first term alone, which motivates the second term to contrain the solution space. 

## The probability model

The probability model $p(x)$ can be expressed in terms of two other model distributions: the source distribution $p(s)$ and the likelihood $p(x | s)$.  

Term (1) uses a (sliced) Wasserstein divergence. Computing this divergence requires data samples and samples from the model in two steps:  $s \sim p(s)$ then $x | s \sim p(x | s)$. Differentiating through this divergence requires the probability model, and therefore $p(s)$ and $p(x | s)$, to be samplable and the sampling process should be differentiable. 

Term (2) uses a reverse KL divergence. Computing this divergence requires evaluating the densities of the model and reference distributions, as well as samples from the model in two steps:  $s \sim p(s)$ then $x | s \sim p(x | s)$. Differentiating through this divergence requires the probability model, and therefore $p(s)$ and $p(x | s)$, to be samplable and the sampling process should be differentiable. Additionally, the model density should be known or else a differentiable approximation of the divergence (the authors use the Kozachenko-Leonenko estimator) should be available. 

## Numerics

The authors validate their method using synthetic data where the source is known, and real data where the observations are neurophyisiological recordings and the likelihood is given by a mechanistic model.

Strengths:
The presentation is clear and well-explained.

Weaknesses:
Overall, I have two concerns. 

1. The computational constraints on the model are heavy

The choices of divergences (Sliced-Wasserstein and reverse KL) in the cost function impose a number of computational constraints of the model.

Term 1 in the cost uses a vanilla Sliced-Wasserstein divergence estimator that is sample-based. This means the likelihood model $p(x | s)$ should be samplable and the sampling process should be differentiable. The authors propose to train a differentiable approximator of a black-box simulator. To be fair, the authors acknowledge that this is a general issue beyond their paper. 

Term 2 in the cost uses a vanilla KL divergence estimator which requires knowing the density of the model, but the authors circumvent this by using another estimator named "Kozachenko-Leonenko". Given that the authors already used a sample-based divergence in Term 1, wouldn't using another sample-based divergence in the Term 2 (e.g. the Sliced-Wasserstein again, or an MMD) between the model and reference distributions not add any further computational constraints on the model?

2. The evaluation procedure or goal remains unclear to me

The framework of minimizing a cost made up of a reconstruction and regularization term is well-studied in optimization. Once could imagine two natural evaluation procedures:
- how well the true sources are recovered? But the authors are clear that designing an identifiable probability model where the sources can be recovered (up to acceptable indeterminacies) is not the goal here. 
- how efficient is their method on a new test dataset, as compared to other similar methods?

It seems to be that the authors answer the latter, but it is not clear to me what are the other methods to benchmark against.

Questions:
Could the authors address the weaknesses part?

Limitations:
Yes.

Rating:
6

This paper introduces a new algorithm for source estimation, the task of estimating a model's parameters probability distributions consistent with observations. In opposition to previous algorithms, the proposed method is sampling-based and can hence be used when the model is a simulator, implicitly defining the likelihood even if the observations are high-dimensional. The authors highlight that several probability distributions might be consistent with the observations and advocate favoring the one with the highest entropy. Their method provides a way to target high-entropy solutions and shows that this new objective leads to a unique optimum.

Strengths:
### Originality
* The method is new to me.

### Quality

* The method is evaluated on a wide variety of benchmarks, and several runs have been made. The experiments showcase that the entropy regularization indeed leads to higher entropy sources.

### Clarity
* All the necessary details are provided
* The paper is well articulated.
* The method is clearly explained
* It is clearly stated how the method fits in the literature. 
* Figures are clean.

* Source estimation is a task that has received attention in recent years.
* This paper fills a gap in the literature by providing a method that works with high-dimensional simulators.

Weaknesses:
### Originality
* I have no concerns regarding originality.

### Quality
* I find that the Lotka-Volterra and SIR experiments are not totally convincing. First, it lacks comparison with NEB, which is claimed to perform poorly in such settings. I agree that NEB will probably provide poor results, but this should still be shown empirically, in my opinion. Second, the Wasserstein distance is hard to interpret. reviews.text:
# Expanded Review: Sourcerer: Sample-based Maximum Entropy Source Distribution Estimation

## Summary

This paper introduces Sourcerer, a novel algorithm for source distribution estimation—the task of inferring a distribution over model parameters that is consistent with a dataset of observations. The key innovation of this work is that the proposed method is purely sample-based, making it applicable to simulators with intractable likelihoods and high-dimensional observation spaces. A central theoretical contribution is the observation that the source distribution estimation problem can be ill-posed, as multiple parameter distributions may produce identical observational distributions when passed through a simulator. To address this fundamental issue, the authors propose targeting the maximum entropy distribution among all valid source distributions, thereby obtaining a unique, well-defined solution. The method leverages the Sliced-Wasserstein distance as a differentiable, sample-based distance metric between distributions, avoiding the need for explicit likelihood evaluations. The authors validate their approach across diverse benchmarks and demonstrate its utility on a contemporary scientific problem: inferring source distributions for Hodgkin-Huxley neuron model parameters from experimental electrophysiological recordings.

## Strengths

### Originality

* The method is novel and presents a fresh perspective on the source distribution estimation problem. The connection between the ill-posedness of source estimation and the maximum entropy principle is well-motivated and appears to be original to this work.
* The combination of maximum entropy principles with sample-based optimization using Sliced-Wasserstein distance is creative and represents a meaningful departure from existing likelihood-based approaches such as Neural Empirical Bayes.
* The paper clearly positions its contributions relative to prior work in empirical Bayes, simulation-based inference, and related fields, demonstrating awareness of the broader context while establishing novelty.

### Quality

* The mathematical derivations are sound. Proposition 2.1 establishes the uniqueness of the maximum entropy source distribution under appropriate assumptions, and the proof provided in Appendix A.7 is rigorous and complete. The convexity argument and strict concavity of entropy are correctly applied.
* The regularized objective in Equation (3) provides a principled approximation to the constrained optimization problem in Equation (2), and the connection to Lagrangian relaxation and penalty methods is appropriately discussed.
* The experimental evaluation is comprehensive and well-designed. The paper includes multiple runs (five independent runs) for all experiments, with error bars and standard deviations reported in both tables and figures, allowing for proper assessment of variability and statistical significance.
* The benchmark suite is diverse, spanning low-dimensional tasks (Two Moons, Inverse Kinematics) to higher-dimensional problems (Lotka-Volterra, SIR), and includes both differentiable simulators and learned surrogates. This breadth demonstrates the generality of the approach.
* The comparison with Neural Empirical Bayes (NEB) on the benchmark tasks is thorough and fair. The authors use the same source model parameterization (neural samplers) and provide comparable computational resources, ensuring a meaningful comparison.
* The entropy regularization clearly demonstrates its intended effect: across all benchmark tasks, entropy is substantially increased without sacrificing data consistency (Table 1), with C2ST accuracies remaining close to 50% across all conditions.
* The ablation studies exploring the dependence on the final regularization strength λ (Figure 3b) are informative, revealing a sharp but interpretable trade-off between data fidelity and entropy gains.
* The inclusion of supplementary experiments with reduced dataset sizes (N=100 for Two Moons) and higher-dimensional parameter spaces (D=25 for Gaussian Mixture) in Figure A5 strengthens claims about robustness.
* The Hodgkin-Huxley application is compelling, demonstrating the method's applicability to real neuroscientific data with 1033 experimental recordings and 13 simulator parameters. The visual inspection of simulated voltage traces (Figure 5a) and the distribution matching of summary statistics (Figure 5b) provide qualitative validation that complements quantitative metrics.

### Clarity

* The paper is exceptionally well-written with clear exposition of both the motivation and technical content. The problem statement in Section 2.1 is concise yet comprehensive, establishing both the ill-posedness of source estimation and the proposed solution.
* All necessary algorithmic details are provided. Algorithm 1 gives clear pseudocode, and the appendices contain extensive implementation details including network architectures (A.4), training procedures (A.3), and hyperparameter choices.
* The figures are of high quality and effectively communicate key results. Figure 1 provides an intuitive illustration of the ill-posedness problem and the maximum entropy solution. Figure 2 gives a clear schematic of the method pipeline. Figure 3 effectively shows the entropy-fidelity trade-off across benchmark tasks.
* The connection to existing literature is clearly articulated. Section 4 provides a comprehensive discussion of related work in empirical Bayes, simulation-based inference, and generalized Bayesian inference, with explicit discussion of how Sourcerer differs from and complements these approaches.
* The paper clearly explains the choice of Sliced-Wasserstein distance (Section 2.3), justifying it on the basis of computational efficiency (O(n log n) vs O(n³) for standard Wasserstein) while noting its convergence properties.
* The use of the Kozachenko-Leonenko entropy estimator is well-justified and clearly explained, with appropriate acknowledgment that this is one choice among several possible sample-based entropy estimators.

### Significance

* Source distribution estimation is an important problem with applications across multiple scientific disciplines, as evidenced by the diverse terminology used (unfolding in particle physics, population of models in electrophysiology, population inference in gravitational wave astronomy). This work makes a meaningful contribution to a recognized problem area.
* The paper addresses a genuine gap in the literature by providing a method that scales to high-dimensional observation spaces without requiring surrogate likelihood models. This is a significant practical advantage over likelihood-based methods.
* The theoretical insight that the maximum entropy principle provides a principled resolution to the ill-posedness of source estimation is conceptually important and may inspire future work in this area.
* The application to Hodgkin-Huxley models demonstrates that the method can be applied to contemporary scientific problems. Parameter inference for biophysical models is an active area of neuroscience research, and methods that account for parameter degeneracy (as highlighted in the paper) are valuable.
* The work has potential downstream impact for Bayesian inference tasks. As noted in the paper, estimated source distributions can serve as informative priors for posterior inference on individual observations, potentially improving inference efficiency and robustness.

## Weaknesses

### Quality Issues

* **Limited comparison on high-dimensional tasks**: The Lotka-Volterra and SIR experiments presented in Section 3.2 lack direct comparison with NEB, which is a significant limitation. While the authors acknowledge that NEB "probably" performs poorly on these tasks due to the need for surrogate likelihood models, this should be demonstrated empirically rather than assumed. The paper states that "we train surrogate models with a reduced dimensionality" for NEB comparison (Appendix A.5), but the results are relegated to supplementary material and are not prominently discussed in the main text. This comparison should be more prominent and clearly presented in the main results section (Section 3.2) to substantiate the claimed advantage of Sourcerer over NEB for high-dimensional problems.

* **Interpretation of Wasserstein distances on high-dimensional tasks**: The Sliced-Wasserstein distances reported in Figure 4 are difficult to interpret in isolation. For the SIR model (Figure 4a), the paper reports that "the Sliced-Wasserstein distance between simulations and observations is close to the minimum expected distance," but without explicit numerical values or comparison to NEB results, it is not immediately clear whether this distance should be considered "good." The minimum expected distance is computed as the distance between simulations of different sets of samples from the same original source, but this baseline itself depends on dataset size and sampling variability. More concrete interpretation would strengthen the claims. For instance, reporting the ratio of achieved distance to minimum expected distance, or providing explicit numerical comparisons to NEB's performance, would make these results more convincing. The paper would benefit from including quantitative comparisons with NEB results on the reduced-dimensionality surrogates in the main text of Section 3.2, not just in the appendix.

* **Surrogate training details and their impact**: While the paper discusses surrogate training in the appendices, the potential impact of surrogate quality on source estimation is not thoroughly analyzed. For high-dimensional tasks, the quality of the surrogate model could significantly affect the final source estimate. The paper trains surrogates on 10⁶ simulations for the Lotka-Volterra and SIR models, but does not provide diagnostics of surrogate quality (e.g., surrogate error metrics) or sensitivity analyses showing how source estimation results vary with surrogate quality. This is particularly important given that the paper claims advantages over methods that require explicit likelihoods, yet still requires

They propose to use a sample-based technique that optimizes for the Sliced-Wasserstein distance measuring the discrepancy between the simulated data and the real data. They propose experiments on both synthetic and real-world models.

- The method is simple, but principled and effective on both synthetic and realistic data 
- The paper represents an interesting contribution to the field of source estimation methods.
reviews.weaknesses:
I do not see any major weakness in the work. A minor one:

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

The authors propose a principled approach to source distribution estimation by incorporating the maximum entropy principle to resolve the inherent ill-posedness of the problem. Rather than relying on likelihood-based methods that require tractable likelihoods, they propose a sample-based technique that optimizes the Sliced-Wasserstein distance to measure the discrepancy between simulated and observed data. The method is evaluated on both synthetic benchmark tasks and a realistic application to the Hodgkin-Huxley model of neuronal dynamics, demonstrating consistent improvements in source distribution entropy without sacrificing simulation fidelity.

## Strengths

**Clarity and Presentation**: The paper is exceptionally well-written with clear conceptual exposition and logical flow. The authors present the problem formulation in Section 2 with careful motivation, beginning with the ill-posed nature of source distribution estimation (illustrated compellingly in Figure 1) and then introducing the maximum entropy principle as a natural solution. The progression from the constrained optimization problem (Eq. 2) to the practical regularized objective (Eq. 3) is particularly well-motivated. The experimental section is organized in a way that builds intuition progressively, starting with benchmark tasks before moving to high-dimensional problems and real-world applications. Concepts are presented consequentially, making it easy to follow both the methodological contributions and experimental validation.

**Principled and Effective Methodology**: The approach is elegantly simple yet theoretically grounded. Proposition 2.1 provides a solid theoretical foundation by establishing both the existence and uniqueness of the maximum entropy source distribution under reasonable assumptions. The proof in Appendix A.7 is rigorous and the convexity argument is particularly insightful. Beyond the theoretical contribution, the practical instantiation of the method is well-designed. The use of the Sliced-Wasserstein distance is well-justified given its computational efficiency (O(n log n) complexity compared to O(n³) for standard Wasserstein distance) and its suitability for sample-based optimization. The dynamic scheduling of the regularization parameter λ (initialized at 1 and decayed linearly) is a clever practical contribution that encourages the method to first explore high-entropy regions before tightening data-consistency constraints.

**Comprehensive Experimental Validation**: The experimental evaluation is thorough and demonstrates the method's effectiveness across diverse settings. The benchmark results in Table 1 show that Sourcerer consistently achieves C2ST accuracies comparable to the Neural Empirical Bayes baseline (around 50%, indicating good data-consistency) while substantially increasing source entropy. For instance, on the SLCP task, the method achieves entropy of 9.81 ± 0.039 with regularization compared to -0.36 ± 0.095 for NEB. The experiments on high-dimensional simulators (Section 3.2) are particularly valuable, demonstrating that the method can handle 50-100 dimensional observation spaces directly without requiring likelihood surrogates, a capability that NEB lacks. The real-world application to the Hodgkin-Huxley model (Section 3.3) with 1033 electrophysiological recordings provides compelling evidence of practical utility. Visual inspection of Figure 5a shows that simulations from the estimated source are qualitatively similar to experimental recordings, avoiding the unrealistic properties (e.g., premature spiking) present in uniform prior simulations.

**Meaningful Contribution to the Field**: This work represents a significant advance in source distribution estimation methods. The explicit incorporation of the maximum entropy principle addresses a fundamental issue that has been acknowledged but not thoroughly resolved in prior work (as noted in Vandegar et al. [58]). The sample-based approach using Sliced-Wasserstein distance opens the method to a broader class of simulators than likelihood-based approaches, as discussed in Section 2.4. This is a meaningful practical contribution, as many scientific simulators lack tractable likelihoods. The ability to work directly with differentiable simulators without requiring surrogate models (demonstrated in Section 3.2) is particularly valuable for high-dimensional problems.

## Weaknesses

**W1: Limited Analysis of Scalability to Very High Dimensions**: While the paper demonstrates competence on problems up to 100-dimensional observation spaces (Lotka-Volterra and SIR models), a more thorough investigation of scalability to even higher dimensionality would strengthen the contribution. The current experiments show results for D=2, 4, 5, 8, and up to 100-dimensional observation spaces, but it would be valuable to understand at what point the method begins to struggle. For instance, how does the Kozachenko-Leonenko entropy estimator (Appendix A.6) perform in very high dimensions? The authors acknowledge in Appendix A.5 that they tested robustness to increased dimensionality (GM task with D=25), but a more systematic study of how performance scales with dimension would be informative. Does the method maintain its entropy-fidelity trade-off in, say, 500 or 1000-dimensional spaces? Such analysis would clarify the practical limits of applicability and help practitioners determine when alternative approaches might be necessary.

## Questions and Suggestions

**Q1: Framing of Maximum Entropy as Universally Beneficial**: In several places, the authors present the maximum entropy principle as an intrinsically beneficial objective (e.g., in the abstract: "prioritizes retaining as much uncertainty as possible" and in Section 2: "choosing the 'maximum ignorance' distribution"). While the authors do discuss the use of reference distributions to modulate this principle (Section 2.2, Eq. 4), some statements that position maximum entropy as a sort of gold standard could be toned down. For instance, the paper could more explicitly acknowledge that maximum entropy is a choice among principled alternatives, not a universally optimal criterion. The framing in Eq. (4) with KL divergence regularization relative to a reference distribution is actually more nuanced and context-dependent than the abstract might suggest. Some applications might benefit from other regularization principles (e.g., Jeffrey's prior, mentioned briefly in the limitations) that prioritize different notions of "reasonable" prior beliefs. A more balanced presentation would acknowledge that the maximum entropy principle is particularly well-motivated in the absence of prior information, but that practitioners should consider alternative principles when domain knowledge or specific desiderata suggest different choices.

**Q2: Sensitivity to Distance Metric Choice**: The paper addresses this briefly in the limitations section (Section 5) and provides an appendix showing results with Maximum Mean Discrepancy (Figure A4), but a more thorough investigation of how different distance metrics affect the estimated source distributions would be valuable. The authors correctly note that "different distance metrics can lead to different estimated sources, depending on its sensitivity to different features." However, the paper would benefit from deeper analysis of this phenomenon. For instance:
  - How sensitive are the entropy and C2ST accuracy results to the choice between SWD and MMD across all benchmark tasks?
  - Are there cases where SWD and MMD lead to qualitatively different source distributions?
  - Could practitioners use ensemble approaches or multiple distance metrics to obtain more robust estimates?
  - For the Hodgkin-Huxley application, how would results change if different distance metrics were used on different subsets of the summary statistics?
  
The authors mention that "it might be appropriate to use a combination of several distance metrics for different modalities of the data" but don't explore this empirically. Such investigation would provide practical guidance for practitioners and strengthen the methodological contribution.

**Q3: Multiple Loss Functions for Parameter-Specific Constraints**: An interesting extension the authors could discuss is the case where different parameters or parameter subsets may require different distance metrics or regularization strategies. In the Hodgkin-Huxley application, for example, different ion channel parameters might have different biological constraints or might affect different aspects of the neural response. Could the framework be extended to use multiple loss terms, each targeting different parameter subsets or different aspects of the data? For instance, one could imagine using one distance metric for spike timing fidelity and another for voltage amplitude characteristics. The authors briefly mention this possibility ("it might be appropriate to use a combination of several distance metrics for different modalities of the data") but don't develop it. This would be particularly relevant for complex biological models where different parameters have different interpretability and importance.

## Limitations

The authors provide a thorough and honest discussion of limitations in Section 5, addressing the key practical challenges of their approach. They correctly identify the dependence on distance metric selection, the choice of regularization strength λ, the requirement for differentiable simulators (necessitating surrogates for discrete simulators), and the choice to enforce maximum entropy on the entire source distribution. These limitations are inherent to the problem setting and methodological choices rather than oversights. The paper's acknowledgment that "there is still an onus on the practitioner to carefully select a reasonable distance metric for the data at hand" is particularly important and appropriately transparent. The authors also note that while they demonstrate robustness to λ over a range of values (Figure 3b), "principled methods for defining the regularization strength are desirable." This is an honest
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors propose to use the maximum entropy principle (possibly tempered with a prior) in order to reduce the ambiguity in solving the problem of source distribution estimation. They propose to use a sample-based technique that optimizes for the Sliced-Wasserstein distance measuring the discrepancy between the simulated data and the real data. They propose experiments on both synthetic and real-world models.

Strengths:
- The paper is extremely clear, concepts are presented consequentially and the method and experiments are easy to follow
- The method is simple, but principled and effective on both synthetic and realistic data 
- The paper represents an interesting contribution to the field of source estimation methods.

Weaknesses:
I do not see any major weakness in the work. A minor one:

- W1: it would be interesting to see a more thorough study for models with even higher dimensionality, and how the proposed method's performance scales.

Questions:
- Q1: In some lines authors frame as the maximum entropy principle as something intrinsically beneficial. As they already discuss, this can be modulated by using a reference distribution. Maybe some statements that make the maximum entropy principle stand out as a sort of gold standard could be toned down a bit?
- Q2: Would it be possible for the authors to show what could happen with the choice of other or wrong distance types?
- Q3: An interesting case the authors could discuss is when multiple losses may be required for different subsets of parameters?

Limitations:
The authors already address the limitations, or the limitations are intrinsic to the field and cannot be attributed to the proposed methodology.

Rating:
7

reviews.summary: The paper introduces “Sourcerer,” a new method for source distribution estimation, focusing on maximum entropy distributions to effectively handle ill-posed problems common in simulating scientific phenomena. This approach leverages the Sliced-Wasserstein distance for sample-based evaluation, offering a significant advantage for simulators with intractable likelihoods, and is demonstrated to recover high-entropy source distributions without sacrificing simulation fidelity across various tasks.
reviews.strengths:
- Utilizing the Sliced-Wasserstein distance for evaluating distribution discrepancies enables the method to operate effectively with simulators that have intractable likelihoods.

- The approach is rigorously tested across multiple scenarios, including high-dimensional observation spaces and complex simulators. The results demonstrate that it not only maintains high fidelity in reproducing the empirical distributions but also achieves higher entropy in estimated source distributions compared to existing methods.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The proposed method only applies to differential simulators. I know a lot of these problems in computational biology have non differentiable simulators, as well as the Ising models. This is the drawback of the proposed method.

- I also wish there is more theoretical results in terms of the statistical consistency and error bounds of using the sliced Wasserstein distance. Because the sliced Wasserstein distance is approximated via empirical measures. The estimation error is highly depending on the sample size.

- I'm not sure if the maximum entropy approach is aligned with the intuition. What if the source distribution is highly concentrated in an area with high prob mass? I know $\lambda$ can be tuned to control it but will it affect the original problem a lot?

- Is there any principle of how to choose the hyperparameter $\lambda$?

- I know the sliced Wasserstein distance is not really informative because it is estimated via samples thus the density information is a sort of missing. Is there any empirical result of the convergence speeds for the proposed method vs the benchmark NEB?

Summary:
The paper introduces “Sourcerer,” a new method for source distribution estimation, focusing on maximum entropy distributions to effectively handle ill-posed problems common in simulating scientific phenomena. This approach leverages the Sliced-Wasserstein distance for sample-based evaluation, offering a significant advantage for simulators with intractable likelihoods, and is demonstrated to recover high-entropy source distributions without sacrificing simulation fidelity across various tasks.

Strengths:
- Utilizing the Sliced-Wasserstein distance for evaluating distribution discrepancies enables the method to operate effectively with simulators that have intractable likelihoods.

- The approach is rigorously tested across multiple scenarios, including high-dimensional observation spaces and complex simulators. The results demonstrate that it not only maintains high fidelity in reproducing the empirical distributions but also achieves higher entropy in estimated source distributions compared to existing methods.

Weaknesses:
- The proposed method only applies to differential simulators. I know a lot of these problems in computational biology have non differentiable simulators, as well as the Ising models. This is the drawback of the proposed method.

- I also wish there is more theoretical results in terms of the statistical consistency and error bounds of using the sliced Wasserstein distance. Because the sliced Wasserstein distance is approximated via empirical measures. The estimation error is highly depending on the sample size.

- I'm not sure if the maximum entropy approach is aligned with the intuition. What if the source distribution is highly concentrated in an area with high prob mass? I know $\lambda$ can be tuned to control it but will it affect the original problem a lot?

Questions:
- what is the motivation of using the sliced Wasserstein distance? There are other types of distances can be used for this problem. For example, the maximum-mean discrepancy (MMD) and the kernelized Stein discrepancy (KSD). MMD and KSD also have their sliced versions.

- Is there any principle of how to choose the hyperparameter $\lambda$?

- I know the sliced Wasserstein distance is not really informative because it is estimated via samples thus the density information is a sort of missing. Is there any empirical result of the convergence speeds for the proposed method vs the benchmark NEB?

# Expanded Peer Review: Sourcerer: Sample-based Maximum Entropy Source Distribution Estimation

## Summary

The paper introduces "Sourcerer," a novel method for source distribution estimation that addresses the ill-posed nature of this problem by targeting maximum entropy distributions. The key innovation lies in combining the maximum entropy principle with the Sliced-Wasserstein distance (SWD) to enable sample-based optimization without requiring tractable likelihoods. The authors demonstrate their approach across diverse tasks ranging from benchmark simulators to a real-world application involving the Hodgkin-Huxley model applied to electrophysiological recordings. The method consistently recovers source distributions with substantially higher entropy than recent competitors, particularly the Neural Empirical Bayes (NEB) baseline, while maintaining high fidelity in reproducing empirical data distributions.

## Strengths

**1. Principled approach to handling ill-posedness**

The paper makes a valuable theoretical contribution by formally addressing the ill-posedness of source distribution estimation through Proposition 2.1, which establishes the uniqueness of the maximum entropy solution under appropriate conditions. This provides a principled mathematical foundation for the approach, distinguishing it from ad-hoc regularization schemes. The proof in Appendix A.7 is rigorous and clearly demonstrates why maximum entropy is a natural choice for resolving non-uniqueness in source distributions. The illustrative example of the absolute value function (x = |θ|) effectively demonstrates the practical relevance of this ill-posedness problem.

**2. Sample-based methodology enables broader applicability**

Utilizing the Sliced-Wasserstein distance for evaluating distribution discrepancies represents a significant methodological advance. The approach enables the method to operate effectively with simulators that have intractable likelihoods, removing a critical bottleneck that limits empirical Bayes methods. Unlike NEB, which requires explicit likelihood evaluation and differentiability of the likelihood function, Sourcerer only requires that sampling from the simulator is differentiable. This is an important practical distinction that extends applicability to a wider class of scientific simulators. The authors demonstrate this advantage particularly clearly in Section 3.2 with the deterministic SIR model, where traditional likelihood-based approaches would struggle or fail entirely.

**3. Comprehensive experimental validation across diverse domains**

The experimental evaluation is thorough and well-designed. The paper includes four benchmark tasks (Two Moons, Inverse Kinematics, SLCP, Gaussian Mixture) that allow direct comparison with NEB, followed by high-dimensional experiments (Lotka-Volterra with 100-dimensional observations, SIR with 50-dimensional observations) that showcase scalability advantages. The real-world application to Hodgkin-Huxley parameter inference from 1033 electrophysiological recordings demonstrates practical utility. Across all these diverse settings, the method consistently achieves C2ST accuracies close to 50% (indicating good data-consistency) while substantially increasing source entropy compared to baselines. The numerical results in Table 1 show entropy improvements ranging from approximately 0.2 to 2 nats across different tasks.

**4. Effective handling of high-dimensional observation spaces**

The method's ability to work directly with high-dimensional differentiable simulators without requiring likelihood surrogates is a genuine advantage. In Section 3.2, the authors demonstrate this by performing source estimation directly on the SIR model (50-dimensional time series) and Lotka-Volterra model (100-dimensional time series) without first training surrogate models. This contrasts favorably with NEB's approach, which requires training normalizing flow surrogates and achieves worse C2ST accuracies (0.62 vs 0.56 for Lotka-Volterra). For practitioners working with high-dimensional simulators, this represents a significant practical advantage.

**5. Robustness and flexibility in distance metric selection**

The paper demonstrates that the method is not overly dependent on the specific choice of distance metric. Figure A4 shows that using Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) produces comparable results to SWD across the benchmark tasks, suggesting the approach is reasonably robust to this design choice. The authors also acknowledge in the limitations section that practitioners should carefully select appropriate distance metrics for their specific applications, showing awareness of this important consideration.

## Weaknesses

**1. Restriction to differentiable simulators significantly limits applicability**

A fundamental limitation of the proposed method is its requirement for differentiable simulators. While the authors acknowledge this in the limitations section (Section 5), the practical implications deserve more thorough discussion. Many scientific simulators in computational biology, including agent-based models, discrete-event simulators, and stochastic simulators with discrete components, are inherently non-differentiable. Examples mentioned by the reviewer—such as Ising models and various computational biology simulators—represent important application domains where this method cannot be directly applied. 

The paper addresses this partially through surrogate modeling (Section 2.4), but this introduces additional complexity and potential sources of error. The surrogate training itself requires careful tuning and introduces approximation error that can propagate through the source estimation process. For instance, in the Hodgkin-Huxley application, the authors train a multi-layer perceptron surrogate on 10^6 simulations, which itself represents a substantial computational investment. While the authors note that surrogates modeling only implicit likelihoods are acceptable (unlike NEB), the surrogate training still dominates computational cost (approximately 20 minutes for Hodgkin-Huxley vs. 30 seconds for source estimation; see Table A1). For practitioners with non-differentiable simulators, this represents a significant practical barrier that deserves more extensive discussion and potentially empirical investigation of how surrogate quality affects final source estimation results.

**2. Insufficient theoretical analysis of statistical properties**

The paper lacks theoretical results regarding the statistical consistency and convergence rates of the proposed estimator. While the authors provide Proposition 2.1 establishing uniqueness of the maximum entropy solution, they do not provide convergence guarantees or error bounds for the regularized objective (Equation 3) that is actually optimized in practice. This is a significant gap because:

- The Sliced-Wasserstein distance is estimated from finite samples, and its estimation error depends on sample size with rate O(√n) as noted in the paper. However, how this estimation error propagates to the final source distribution estimate is not analyzed.
- The regularized objective (Equation 3) is an approximation to the constrained problem (Equation 2), and the authors mention this connection to penalty methods in Appendix A.7 but do not provide formal convergence analysis.
- The Kozachenko-Leonenko entropy estimator used in practice is a biased estimator (particularly in high dimensions), but the impact of this bias on the final results is not quantified.

These theoretical gaps are particularly concerning given that the paper makes strong claims about the superiority of the maximum entropy approach. A more rigorous analysis would strengthen the contribution significantly. For instance, what sample complexity is required to achieve a given level of accuracy in source distribution estimation? How does this compare to empirical Bayes approaches? The authors should either provide such theoretical results or more clearly delineate the boundary between what is proven and what is empirically validated.

**3. Alignment between maximum entropy principle and scientific intuition remains unclear**

While the maximum entropy principle is mathematically elegant and resolves the ill-posedness problem, the paper does not thoroughly discuss whether this principle aligns with scientific intuition in all contexts. The authors acknowledge this concern in the response to reviewer questions but the main text does not adequately address it. Consider a scenario where the true source distribution is highly concentrated in a region with high probability mass—for instance, if biological neurons have parameters that cluster tightly around specific values due to evolutionary constraints. The maximum entropy approach would prefer to spread this distribution more broadly, which could contradict actual biological reality.

The paper argues that the hyperparameter λ can be tuned to control this behavior, but this somewhat undermines the claim that maximum entropy provides a principled solution to the ill-posedness problem. If practitioners must tune λ to incorporate domain knowledge about whether parameters should be concentrated or dispersed, then the maximum entropy principle is being overridden by external considerations. The paper would benefit from more extensive discussion of when maximum entropy is appropriate and when alternative principles (such as maximum a posteriori estimation with informative priors) might be preferable. The Hodgkin-Huxley application (Section 3.3) provides some evidence that the entropy regularization is beneficial, but more systematic investigation of this trade-off would strengthen the contribution.

**4. Limited principled guidance for hyperparameter selection**

The paper provides no principled methodology for selecting the final regularization strength λ. The authors observe empirically (Figure 3b) that results are "robust to a large range of λ" below a critical threshold, but this is not a satisfactory solution for practitioners. The paper states: "Principled methods for defining the regularization strength are desirable, though as we demonstrate, our results are robust to a large range of λ" (Section 5). However, "robust" is relative—the entropy can vary substantially

reviews.summary: This paper proposes an approach to estimating a maximum-entropy source distribution (akin to a prior distribution over simulator parameters) for a given set of observations and simulation model. Their method assumes a differentiable simulator that may be deterministic or stochastic, and it uses neural samplers to approximate the prior and a variational objective that encourages proximity of both the marginal likelihood to the true data distribution and the estimated source distribution to some known prior distribution (which may be uniform, corresponding to entropy regularisation). In particular, proximity to the true data distribution is measured with a sliced Wasserstein distance, due to its fast computation and differentiability (preserving the differentiable pipeline). The authors present experiments on four benchmark tasks, before extending to higher dimensional and more complex examples such as the single-compartment Hodgkin-Huxley model.
reviews.strengths:
*Originality*

The authors consider using a sample based loss to capture mismatch between the simulated and true data distributions, in contrast to using likelihood-based notions of distance. This is useful since simulation models often lack tractable likelihood functions. 

*Quality* 

The experimental section presents good and extensive empirical testing on a number of toy benchmark models, in addition to two additional more complex simulation models. Their approach is also well-motivated via Proposition 2.1.

*Clarity*

The clarity of writing is generally good. There are a few minor errors in the writing that made it not absolutely clear all the way through:
- The last sentence of the first paragraph of Section 1 isn't a full sentence (or, if it is, then it not well-written because I have read it multiple times and could not turn it into a full sentence in my head).
- There is a subscript $\phi$ missing in Equation 4. 

*Significance*

I think this paper will be of some interest to the community and the techniques presented used by practitioners.
reviews.weaknesses: My main concern is that the work presented looks like it is not a large or significant contribution beyond what is already present in the literature. From my perspective, it looks like someone could reasonably argue that the contribution is just to use a different sample-based loss function to capture the discrepancy between the data distribution and simulator's marginal likelihood. It also isn't quite clear to me how exactly the approach that uses the surrogate model (Sur. in Table 1) is different from what is presented in Vandegar et al. (2020).
reviews.questions:
Could the authors provide some more detail and clarification on how their approach differs/relates to/builds on previous works please? Ideally at least those papers already cited such as Vandegar et al. (2020), but I think some additional related literature is also missing, such as references [1] (relevant since it discusses variational approaches to targeting generalised Bayesian posteriors) and [2] (relevant to the problem of source estimation in complex simulation models) below. Is there anything that we lose by using something like the Sliced Wasserstein Distance instead of a likelihood-based discrepancy?

[1] _Knoblauch, J., Jewson, J., & Damoulas, T. (2022). An optimization-centric view on Bayes' rule: Reviewing and generalizing variational inference. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 23(132), 1-109._

[2] _Dyer, J., Quera-Bofarull, A., Bishop, N., Farmer, J. D., Calinescu, A., & Wooldridge, M. (2024, May). Population Synthesis as Scenario Generation for Simulation-based Planning under Uncertainty. In Proceedings of the 23rd International Conference on Autonomous Agents and Multiagent Systems (pp. 490-498)._
reviews.limitations:
The authors provide a good discussion of the limitations towards the end of the paper. Perhaps something additional to discuss would be limitations associated with simulators that involve discrete randomness, which (as far as I can tell) aren't tested in this paper but which often appears in simulation modelling and presents an additional complication when the requirement is that sampling from the simulator is a differentiable operation (see e.g. [1] below). I also do not see any problems related to the broader social impact of this work. 

[1] _Arya, G., Schauer, M., Schäfer, F., & Rackauckas, C. (2022). Automatic differentiation of programs with discrete randomness. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 35, 10435-10447._
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes an approach to estimating a maximum-entropy source distribution (akin to a prior distribution over simulator parameters) for a given set of observations and simulation model. Their method assumes a differentiable simulator that may be deterministic or stochastic, and it uses neural samplers to approximate the prior and a variational objective that encourages proximity of both the marginal likelihood to the true data distribution and the estimated source distribution to some known prior distribution (which may be uniform, corresponding to entropy regularisation). In particular, proximity to the true data distribution is measured with a sliced Wasserstein distance, due to its fast computation and differentiability (preserving the differentiable pipeline). The authors present experiments on four benchmark tasks, before extending to higher dimensional and more complex examples such as the single-compartment Hodgkin-Huxley model.

Strengths:
*Originality*

The authors consider using a sample based loss to capture mismatch between the simulated and true data distributions, in contrast to using likelihood-based notions of distance. This is useful since simulation models often lack tractable likelihood functions. 

*Quality* 

The experimental section presents good and extensive empirical testing on a number of toy benchmark models, in addition to two additional more complex simulation models. Their approach is also well-motivated via Proposition 2.1.

*Clarity*

The clarity of writing is generally good. There are a few minor errors in the writing that made it not absolutely clear all the way through:
- The last sentence of the first paragraph of Section 1 isn't a full sentence (or, if it is, then it not well-written because I have read it multiple times and could not turn it into a full sentence in my head).
- There is a subscript $\phi$ missing in Equation 4. 

*Significance*

I think this paper will be of some interest to the community and the techniques presented used by practitioners.

Weaknesses:
My main concern is that the work presented looks like it is not a large or significant contribution beyond what is already present in the literature. From my perspective, it looks like someone could reasonably argue that the contribution is just to use a different sample-based loss function to capture the discrepancy between the data distribution and simulator's marginal likelihood. It also isn't quite clear to me how exactly the approach that uses the surrogate model (Sur. in Table 1) is different from what is presented in Vandegar et al. (2020).

Questions:
Could the authors provide some more detail and clarification on how their approach differs/relates to/builds on previous works please? Ideally at least those papers already cited such as Vandegar et al. (2020), but I think some additional related literature is also missing, such as references [1] (relevant since it discusses variational approaches to targeting generalised Bayesian posteriors) and [2] (relevant to the problem of source estimation in complex simulation models) below. Is there anything that we lose by using something like the Sliced Wasserstein Distance instead of a likelihood-based discrepancy?

[1] _Knoblauch, J., Jewson, J., & Damoulas, T. (2022). An optimization-centric view on Bayes' rule: Reviewing and generalizing variational inference. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 23(132), 1-109._

In Proceedings of the 23rd International Conference on Autonomous Agents and Multiagent Systems (pp. 490-498)._

Limitations:
The authors provide a good discussion of the limitations towards the end of the paper. Perhaps something additional to discuss would be limitations associated with simulators that involve discrete randomness, which (as far as I can tell) aren't tested in this paper but which often appears in simulation modelling and presents an additional complication when the requirement is that sampling from the simulator is a differentiable operation (see e.g. [1] below). I also do not see any problems related to the broader social impact of this work. 

[1] _Arya, G., Schauer, M., Schäfer, F., & Rackauckas, C. (2022). Automatic differentiation of programs with discrete randomness. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 35, 10435-10447._

# Expanded Peer Review: Sourcerer - Sample-based Maximum Entropy Source Distribution Estimation

## Summary

This paper proposes Sourcerer, an approach to estimating a maximum-entropy source distribution (akin to a prior distribution over simulator parameters) for a given set of observations and a simulation model. The method targets the maximum entropy distribution among all source distributions that are consistent with observed data, thereby resolving the ill-posedness of the source distribution estimation problem. The authors' approach assumes a differentiable simulator that may be deterministic or stochastic, and employs neural samplers to approximate the source distribution. A key innovation is the use of a variational objective that encourages both proximity of the simulated data distribution to the true observed distribution and consistency with a reference distribution (which may be uniform, corresponding to entropy regularization). Importantly, proximity to the true data distribution is measured using the Sliced-Wasserstein distance (SWD), chosen for its fast computation, differentiability, and ability to operate in a sample-based manner without requiring tractable likelihoods. The authors present comprehensive experiments on four benchmark tasks, before extending to higher-dimensional and more complex examples such as the single-compartment Hodgkin-Huxley model from electrophysiology.

## Strengths

### Originality

The authors consider using a sample-based loss to capture mismatch between the simulated and true data distributions, in contrast to using likelihood-based notions of distance. This is a valuable contribution since simulation models often lack tractable likelihood functions, which severely limits the applicability of existing empirical Bayes approaches. The use of Sliced-Wasserstein distance as the data-consistency term is particularly clever: it is differentiable with respect to the source model parameters, admits efficient computation via order statistics (O(n log n) complexity as noted in Section 2.3), and converges quickly to its true value with rate O(1/√n). This design choice allows gradients to flow through the entire optimization pipeline, enabling direct optimization through complex simulators without the need for explicit likelihood surrogates in all cases.

The framing of the problem through the maximum entropy principle is also well-motivated and principled. Rather than treating entropy regularization as an ad-hoc addition, the authors ground it in Proposition 2.1, which establishes that the maximum entropy source distribution is unique when it exists. This provides a strong theoretical justification for their approach and distinguishes it from previous work that may have used entropy penalties somewhat arbitrarily. The connection to constrained optimization and the use of a dynamical schedule for λ (decaying from 1 to a final value) is also well-thought-out, as it encourages the variational source model to first explore high-entropy distributions before tightening consistency with the data.

### Quality

The experimental section presents extensive and careful empirical testing across multiple domains and problem settings. The authors evaluate their method on four benchmark tasks (Two Moons, Inverse Kinematics, Simple Likelihood Complex Posterior, and Gaussian Mixture) that span different characteristics: some with low-dimensional parameter spaces, others with higher-dimensional observation spaces, and varying degrees of likelihood complexity. Importantly, for these benchmark tasks, the authors can evaluate their method both on the original differentiable simulators and on learned surrogates, providing a thorough assessment of robustness.

The results in Table 1 are particularly compelling: across all four benchmark tasks, Sourcerer achieves C2ST (Classifier Two Sample Test) accuracies comparable to or better than Neural Empirical Bayes (NEB), while simultaneously achieving substantially higher entropy in the estimated source distributions. For instance, on the Inverse Kinematics task, Sourcerer achieves entropy of 9.81 ± 0.039 with entropy regularization compared to NEB's 7.23 ± 0.052, while maintaining similar C2ST accuracy. This demonstrates that the method does not sacrifice data-consistency for entropy gains—a crucial point that validates the core contribution.

The extension to high-dimensional observation spaces (Section 3.2) is particularly valuable and demonstrates a clear advantage over likelihood-based approaches. For the Lotka-Volterra model with 100-dimensional time series data and the SIR model with 50-dimensional outputs, the authors show that their sample-based approach can operate directly on the simulator without requiring surrogate models of the likelihood, which would be challenging to train for such high-dimensional data. The deterministic SIR model result is especially noteworthy, as likelihood-based approaches like NEB cannot handle deterministic simulators at all.

The real-world application to the Hodgkin-Huxley model (Section 3.3) demonstrates the practical utility of the approach. Using a dataset of 1033 electrophysiological recordings from mouse motor cortex, the authors estimate a source distribution over 13 mechanistic parameters of the single-compartment Hodgkin-Huxley model. The visual inspection of simulations (Figure 5a) and the distribution of summary statistics (Figure 5b) show that the estimated source produces realistic spike trains. The C2ST accuracy of approximately 61% indicates good data-consistency, and the entropy regularization yields substantially higher entropy than the baseline (Figure 5d/e). This application is particularly important because the parameter space of Hodgkin-Huxley models is known to be highly degenerate, and estimating a source distribution that captures this degeneracy is valuable for downstream inference tasks.

The approach is also well-motivated via Proposition 2.1, which establishes uniqueness of the maximum entropy source distribution under appropriate assumptions. The proof in Appendix A.7 is rigorous and correct, establishing both that the set of source distributions is convex and that entropy is strictly concave, thereby guaranteeing uniqueness. The authors also provide illustrative examples (e.g., the deterministic |θ| simulator in Appendix A.7) that clearly demonstrate why the source distribution problem can be ill-posed without the maximum entropy principle.

### Clarity

The writing is generally clear and well-organized. The paper progresses logically from motivation, through the mathematical formulation, to the methods, and finally to comprehensive experiments. Figure 1 effectively illustrates the core problem—that multiple distinct source distributions can produce the same data distribution—and motivates the maximum entropy solution. Figure 2 provides a clear schematic of the Sourcerer pipeline, showing how the entropy of the source and the Sliced-Wasserstein distance term are combined.

The mathematical exposition in Section 2 is careful and precise. The progression from the constrained optimization problem (Eq. 2) to the regularized approximation (Eq. 3) is well-justified, with clear explanation of why the constrained formulation may not have solutions in practice (e.g., due to simulator misspecification). The adaptation to handle reference distributions (Section 2.2) is particularly well-explained, with the key insight that for a uniform box prior, minimizing KL divergence is equivalent to maximizing entropy on the bounded domain.

However, there are a few minor issues that slightly detract from the clarity:
- The last sentence of the first paragraph of Section 1 is not a complete sentence. The text reads: "Adopting the terminology of Vandegar et al. [58], we refer to this task as source distribution estimation." This is actually a complete sentence, but earlier in that paragraph, there appears to be incomplete phrasing that could be clarified.
- In Equation 4, there is a subscript φ missing: the loss should explicitly reference q_φ or q_{ϕ} to be fully consistent with the notation introduced earlier.
- While Algorithm 1 provides pseudocode, some notation could be clearer. For instance, the notation D({x1, . . . xn}, {x'1, . . . x'n}) for the Sliced-Wasserstein distance could be made more explicit.

Despite these minor issues, the overall presentation is professional and the paper is generally easy to follow.

### Significance

The paper makes a solid contribution that will be of interest to the simulation-based inference community and practitioners working with complex simulators. The combination of the maximum entropy principle with sample-based distance metrics addresses a real problem: many simulators of scientific and engineering interest lack tractable likelihoods, making existing empirical Bayes approaches inapplicable. By removing the requirement for tractable likelihoods, the method opens up source distribution estimation to a broader class of simulators.

The method's ability to handle high-dimensional observation spaces without training likelihood surrogates is particularly valuable. This is demonstrated convincingly in Section 3.2 with the Lotka-Volterra and SIR models, where the 50-100 dimensional time series data would be challenging to model with a surrogate likelihood. The application to the Hodgkin-Huxley model shows that the approach can scale to realistic neuroscience applications with moderate parameter dimensionality (13 parameters) and complex dynamics.

The principled use of maximum entropy, rather than arbitrary entropy penalties, provides a theoretical foundation that should appeal to researchers who value well-motivated methodology. The connection to constrained optimization and the theoretical guarantee of uniqu
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes an approach to estimating a maximum-entropy source distribution (akin to a prior distribution over simulator parameters) for a given set of observations and simulation model. Their method assumes a differentiable simulator that may be deterministic or stochastic, and it uses neural samplers to approximate the prior and a variational objective that encourages proximity of both the marginal likelihood to the true data distribution and the estimated source distribution to some known prior distribution (which may be uniform, corresponding to entropy regularisation). In particular, proximity to the true data distribution is measured with a sliced Wasserstein distance, due to its fast computation and differentiability (preserving the differentiable pipeline). The authors present experiments on four benchmark tasks, before extending to higher dimensional and more complex examples such as the single-compartment Hodgkin-Huxley model.

Strengths:
*Originality*

The authors consider using a sample based loss to capture mismatch between the simulated and true data distributions, in contrast to using likelihood-based notions of distance. This is useful since simulation models often lack tractable likelihood functions. 

*Quality* 

The experimental section presents good and extensive empirical testing on a number of toy benchmark models, in addition to two additional more complex simulation models. Their approach is also well-motivated via Proposition 2.1.

*Clarity*

The clarity of writing is generally good. There are a few minor errors in the writing that made it not absolutely clear all the way through:
- The last sentence of the first paragraph of Section 1 isn't a full sentence (or, if it is, then it not well-written because I have read it multiple times and could not turn it into a full sentence in my head).
- There is a subscript $\phi$ missing in Equation 4. 

*Significance*

I think this paper will be of some interest to the community and the techniques presented used by practitioners.

Weaknesses:
My main concern is that the work presented looks like it is not a large or significant contribution beyond what is already present in the literature. From my perspective, it looks like someone could reasonably argue that the contribution is just to use a different sample-based loss function to capture the discrepancy between the data distribution and simulator's marginal likelihood. It also isn't quite clear to me how exactly the approach that uses the surrogate model (Sur. in Table 1) is different from what is presented in Vandegar et al. (2020).

Questions:
Could the authors provide some more detail and clarification on how their approach differs/relates to/builds on previous works please? Ideally at least those papers already cited such as Vandegar et al. (2020), but I think some additional related literature is also missing, such as references [1] (relevant since it discusses variational approaches to targeting generalised Bayesian posteriors) and [2] (relevant to the problem of source estimation in complex simulation models) below. Is there anything that we lose by using something like the Sliced Wasserstein Distance instead of a likelihood-based discrepancy?

[1] _Knoblauch, J., Jewson, J., & Damoulas, T. (2022). An optimization-centric view on Bayes' rule: Reviewing and generalizing variational inference. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 23(132), 1-109._

[2] _Dyer, J., Quera-Bofarull, A., Bishop, N., Farmer, J. D., Calinescu, A., & Wooldridge, M. (2024, May). Population Synthesis as Scenario Generation for Simulation-based Planning under Uncertainty. In Proceedings of the 23rd International Conference on Autonomous Agents and Multiagent Systems (pp. 490-498)._

Limitations:
The authors provide a good discussion of the limitations towards the end of the paper. Perhaps something additional to discuss would be limitations associated with simulators that involve discrete randomness, which (as far as I can tell) aren't tested in this paper but which often appears in simulation modelling and presents an additional complication when the requirement is that sampling from the simulator is a differentiable operation (see e.g. [1] below). I also do not see any problems related to the broader social impact of this work. 

[1] _Arya, G., Schauer, M., Schäfer, F., & Rackauckas, C. (2022). Automatic differentiation of programs with discrete randomness. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 35, 10435-10447._

Rating:
5
